book: The Canons of the Council in Trullo.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

metadata.author: The Canons of the Council in Trullo; Often Called The Quinisext Council

title: Canon LXXI.

verses.book: The Canons of the Council in Trullo.
verses.text: Those who are taught the civil laws must not adopt the customs of the Gentiles, nor be induced to go to the theatre, nor to keep what are called Cylestras, nor to wear clothing contrary to the general custom; and this holds good when they begin their training, when they reach its end, and, in short, all the time of its duration.

verses.book: The Canons of the Council in Trullo.
verses.text: If any one from this time shall dare to do contrary to this canon he is to be cut off.

verses.book: The Canons of the Council in Trullo.
The Canons of the Council in Trullo.
verses.text: Whoever devotes himself to the study of law, uses the manner of the Gentiles, going to the theatre, and rolling in the dust, or dressing differently to custom, shall be cut off.

verses.book: The Canons of the Council in Trullo.
verses.text: Liddell and Scott identify καλίστρα with καλινδήθρα , which they define as “a place for horses to roll after exercise,” and note that it is a synonym of ἀλινδήθρα.

verses.book: The Canons of the Council in Trullo.
verses.text: But it is interesting to note that ἀλίνησις is “a rolling in the dust, an exercise in which wrestlers rolled on the ground.”

verses.book: The Canons of the Council in Trullo.
verses.text: Hefele says that Balsamon and Zonaras have not been able rightly to explain what we are to understand by the forbidden “Cylestras,” but I think Johnson is not far out of the way when he translates “nor to meddle with athletic exercises.”
